text.title: WBS Core Principles
text.summary: The four rules that make a WBS valid: deliverable orientation, 100% rule, 8/80 leaf sizing, and mandatory overhead packages.

rule.statement: Name every WBS node as a noun (the output produced), never as a verb (the activity performed).
rule.rationale: Deliverable names remain stable when implementation changes; activity names do not. "User Authentication Module" survives a rewrite; "Build user authentication" does not.

example.note: Verb-based decomposition — breaks when approach changes.
example.code: 1.1 Design authentication 1.2 Code authentication 1.3 Test authentication
1.1 Authentication Module 1.1.1 Registration System 1.1.2 Login / Logout 1.1.3 Password Reset

rule.statement: Every level must capture 100% of the work in its parent — no gaps, no overlaps.
rule.rationale: Gaps create forgotten work that surfaces late. Overlaps create double-counted effort and budget conflicts. The rule is verified by asking: "If all children are complete, is the parent complete?"

rule.statement: Always include the following overhead branches or flag their absence as a defect: Project Management, QA, Deployment, Documentation, Training, Transition.
rule.rationale: Overhead is 15-25% of real project effort. Agents and manual drafters systematically omit it, creating a budget gap from day one.

rule.statement: Leaf nodes (work packages) must have an effort estimate between 8 and 80 hours. Nodes outside this range must be split or merged.
rule.rationale: Below 8 hours the tracking overhead exceeds the tracking value. Above 80 hours the estimate is a guess and dependencies are invisible. The 8/80 rule is a calibration heuristic, not a law — adjust the bounds to match your tracking unit.

rule.statement: Stop decomposing when the 8/80 rule is satisfied; more than 4 levels rarely adds estimate accuracy.
rule.rationale: Deep trees create maintenance overhead and give a false sense of precision without improving schedule or cost predictability.

rule.statement: WBS IDs are append-only: once assigned, a numeric ID (1.2.3) never changes or gets reused, even if the node is deleted.
rule.rationale: Change requests, risk entries, and tasks reference WBS IDs. Renumbering breaks traceability across the whole project record.
